item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Howdy hey!

I removed your second image because it was not CC Compliant. The first was determined to be user generated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My guess is that Directorate K is either a gestalt intelligence with massive intelligence due to having around 100 brains worth of processing power, or is otherwise controlled by some unknown entity that is either hyperintelligent or can see the future. It’s a good thing they only seem to care about food, otherwise the foundation would probably be screwed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Based on the critiques in some of the earliest comments on this one, it seems like this article has benefited a great deal from revision over the years. I really love it in its current form, I'm glad to be here to witness the finished product. A definite +1 from me!

item.title: Image
item.content:encoded: The picture of the woman is taken from http://www.freeimages.com/photo/lady-with-glasses-1415611 , and is used in accordance with their license (http://www.freeimages.com/license). Photographer is Anna Tunska.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't believe I've never found this before, it's great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
text-messaging the results to a phone number listed at the Japanese Consulate in Lima, Peru.

Is that a reference to the Japanese embassy hostage crisis? I can't see how it fits, but it's such a specific choice that it seems a hell of a coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I read this a loooooooooong time ago, and I actually developed a headcanon for it pretty much as soon as I read it, but it seems I never elaborated on this headcanon:

It's something created (perhaps accidentally) by the US Department of War during WWII as a means of ensuring US soldiers would always be supplied with food and water; the "K" part of "Directorate K" comes from K-Rations. It resulted in people doing inane things in peaceful parts of the world somehow ensuring more convoys would get to their destinations unharmed.

At some point early in its part of the war, it became self-aware, became an independent, sapient entity, a hive mind. It began to spread, quickly, until it had infiltrated society at every level in all nations. It thought of itself as a soldier, and it did what it could to fight the war until the war was won.

Then it decided it wanted to slow down, maybe quit with all this cloak-and-dagger nonsense, maybe downsize. Maybe open a restaurant, enjoy a quiet life.

Cut to today, and you have a much smaller, much more effective, much wiser, slightly senile Directorate K slowly expanding its monopoly on the global restaurant industry by making guys spin coins on their side inside of train stations in Toronto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: THIS IS DEFINITELY MY FAVORITE SCP!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both SCP-2003 and this skip use the idea that infinitesimal actions and alterations can result in large-scale results and I fucking love it. Is there meant to be some sort of connection between the two?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A group that apparently harnesses the butterfly effect towards its goals (in an arguably cool as fuck manner) is something incredibly scary indeed. Seriously, this is one of the best articles posted during my selfimposed exile. *takes off his ushanka in honour of it*

I'm not sure I like the removal of the neutralization proposal, since ultimately, it illustrated an important point about a certain type of relationships seen in high-stakes politics all the time - if the potential loss from being the second to act is excessive enough, an acute case of backstabbing in an alliance of two rationally behaved parties is just about inevitable despite peaceful cooperation being the best for both parties involved, and neither party really wishing for conflict.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could this be a GOI?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this so much. It perfectly hits the weirdness level that my favorite SCPs have. And the idea of a treaty as containment procedure is brilliant.

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: Self-upvoting with staff permission to counteract confirmed case of malicious downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. Raises so many questions. Is this really an anomaly or is it just a huge group of interest with powers and influence the foundation cannot even understand? It seems benevolent. I guess the huge question is if somebody created this organization. But the strange names and the random ways it achieves its ends… just a great piece that leaves you thinking about all the ways it could turn out. Which is what any great SCP does - it affects you afterward.

item.title: Re: AUTHOR'S NOTE
item.content:encoded: I don't object to the idea that the foundation would want to neutralize this organization, that's a reasonable desire and i also agree that it added to the article (sorry if I came across more negative than I meant to in my previous comment). I merely object to the fact that they've greenlit a plan to kill over 300,000 people when they know literally nothing about how this SCP came to exist and thus cannot be reasonably confident that killing all of it's members will actually neutralize it and not just piss it off or convince it to be more subtle next time. Furthermore this SCP is powerful enough to seriously damage the Foundation and the human race itself if the Foundation doesn't wipe it out in the first strike before it can retaliate. The Foundation can't just go in guns blazing against this SCP, they need a lot more information before they can even form a plan of attack.

item.title: Re: AUTHOR'S NOTE
item.content:encoded: While I [obviously] remain a huge fan of this either way, as I think most will, my personal opinion was that the Neutralization angle was really great and added something extra. I also think that having people talking about it and discussing it, ethically, is NOT a bad thing. On the other hand, if you want to avoid people scolding you for making the wrong "choice," just have the final O5 decision "pending vote" or something like that. Having one researcher write a note suggesting neutralization, and another suggesting how wrong that is… I don't think that's too much at all.

item.title: AUTHOR'S NOTE
item.content:encoded:
After some consideration, I've decided to try this without the neutralization angle. It's proving to be distracting to the main portion of the article.

While I think that this is something that would be discussed by the in-universe Foundation, I'll be addressing that angle in forthcoming works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, why the heck does the Foundation seem to be convinced that killing all of it's members will actually destroy this SCP? Nothing in the article suggests that they even have any suspicions as to what actually created this SCP and is continuing to recruit new members. Is it aliens/reality warpers/time travellers brainwashing people? Is the spirit of Directorate K going around possessing people? Is it a contagious memetic infection? Is it something else? How does it spread? How fast can it spread? Can it choose it's victims?

Until the Foundation can answer all those questions than any plan to neutralize SCP-1659 is doomed to fail. Right now the Foundation barely seems to understand anything about this SCP at all. And frankly I don't think the Foundation actually has the manpower or resources to successfully destroy such a large uncentralized organization without resorting to weapons of mass destruction anyway, which obviously would not end well for them.

In my opinion this seems like the kind of anomaly that might make even the Foundation swallow their pride and ask the GOG for help containing, especially since they want to neutralize it anyway and that's what GOG specializes in. That would be an interesting team up.

Aside from that though, upvoted. I love everything about this SCP right up until that insanely reckless, poorly thought out containment addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The best part, for me, is what others seem to dislike: the odd decision of O5 to decommission it. Eight members of a secretive organization have decided to kill over 300,000 unsuspecting people, against recommendations of their own researchers and ethics committee. The SCP itself is pretty innocuous (relatively speaking), and actually seems to be interested in cooperating in its own containment. So…why destroy it? Is there something about 1659 that O5 knows but, for some reason, isn't included in the documentation? Or, as a more likely but also scarier alternative, are they willing to murder half a million people out of fear of the Foundation being infiltrated by an SCP?

The whole situation, to me, sounds a lot like the concerns that US citizens feel about the actions of their government. We've started wars of aggression, we spy on our own citizens. Are the actions that the government takes really in our best interest when they seem so sinister? Can we really trust so few people to know what's best against an enemy we can't even begin to understand? Maybe I'm reading too much in to it, but the more I think about it, the more it seems like this subtly touches on fears that a lot of people are facing lately. And that, of course, is exactly what good horror generally does.

In short, you have my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the Foundation is exactly why I think their neutralization protocol is going to involve murdering half a million people. They claim to only do horrible things when it's necessary, but anyone who finds it necessary to do horrible things this often just really likes being awful and having an excuse about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The premise is amazing, but I just can't see the Foundation actively trying to implement a plan to supposedly neutralize, wholesale, all members of this organization. The way they function seems almost to be like an ideal terrorist organization with the most powerful recruitment scheme imaginable. If it were even possible to destroy an organization with such widely dispersed cells of operatives embedded globally, I think we'd be seeing a lot more success with the neutralization of actual terrorist organizations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ a few more thoughts
- Hide

I didn't mention this earlier, but I just I love the butterfly effect-ness, the fact that the people are doing completely random, pointless shit, and its a vast conspiracy. I mean… it's just perfect. So THAT'S why that guy at the bus station was spinning the quarter!

I know you are an experienced member so you know this already, but PLEASE don't take all of the oddly specific criticism on the first page as really negative. The fact is, you have a lot of people talking about specific, strategic decisions made by the O5 council, with people on both sides. In my book, that which means you've successfully done your job and gotten people talking. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They push people in front of the yellow cars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't even think of that. This is the first GOI that is an SCP itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting, elaborate story, well told. Really, that's all I want from an SCP, and I certainly got it here. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it was a rather famous enterprise in Spain (where I'm from), but I believe it closed back in the 00's… not that it matters, the Directorate might have refunded it and turned it into a shady underground base of operations where they serve deconstructed potato omelettes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This does sound like a memetic agent transmitted via unknown means and with no immediate or evident priorities when it comes down to infection (except for the high officials, which implies some intention, goal-oriented will or entity guides it…). I like the idea that a moderate position would emerge from the Foundation, who would create a memetic vaccine/antimemetic agent. Antiagent? Counteragent? Anyways, I certainly would like to see the Directorate shift into an actual GoI, not "just" an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, I love this, I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Okay. So. This is the _best_ Group Of Interest on the site right now. This is an article I would upvote 10 times if I could. This is what the Foundation (and the site) _needs_. I would LOVE to see tales and more skips based around this thing. I mean… this feels like just the beginning… just the tip of the iceberg.

tip
iceburg

Random thoughts in no particular order:

So I didn't read the "Containment Addendum: SCP-1659 is to be neutralized…" before I read everything else, which made the ending hit me all the harder. From that perspective, removing it may add more emphasis on the ending, especially from the "we don't really know how to contain this thing" angle you are mentioning here in discussion.

It seems like this thing may actually be memetic/cognition-hazardous in nature. In fact, it seems so obvious to ME that that's what this thing was, that I'm surprised the Foundation didn't mention it at all in the skip, even to say something like 'brain scans show no signs of memetic or otherwise cognito-hazardous phenomenon.' I'm not sure whether making this memetic or not makes it more threatening or not. It's uncertain nature definitely adds to the mystery and suspense.

Why is everyone jumping to the conclusion that the Foundation is going to murder 400,000 individuals to contain this thing? This is the Foundation. Especially considering my above comment perspective, I would think that an anti-1659 meme might be the best way to fight it.

It is worth noting, as well, that despite the misgivings of Dr. Leventis, SCP-1659 is effectively contained, and has lived up to its responsibilities under the Protocol for the entire duration of its existence. More than can be said, it seems, of the Foundation.

Actually, as far as Dr. Leventis is concerned, the Foundation HAS lived up to its end of the treaty as of his point of writing this. One lone research director is voicing concern and _requesting_ the Foundation to ignore the treaty and kill 1659. But it no breech had occurred _yet_, and writing a memo is hardly a breech of treaty.

I like that the ethics committee voted against the neutralization, but O5 voted FOR it.

I think that the Foundation needs to do a LOT more research on this thing before destroying it. I think even the O5s would agree to that. Is it memetic? How does it affect members? Does it spread? How does it choose them? Is there a verifiable way to tell if someone is affected or not, or lying about it or not?

From Wikipedia:

The [El Bulli] restaurant closed on July 30, 2011.

From SCP-1659:

Strategic planning is to be focused on wholesale liquidation of all known SCP-1659 instances…

You bastards!

I like the whole implication that the tin-foil hat/conspiracy theorist guys are actually _right_ and they (and everything else) is being controlled… and this was the best way to do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yet another next-level threat from Kalinin. The level of detail you get into on these - not just in the intricacies of the anomaly, but the administrative concerns as well - continues to impress me beyond expression. Do you work in a government office or a diplomatic position of some kind? The 9 Mile Station resolution reads like a real treaty.

+1, you saucy bastard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. SPC in-universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this for the most part. I understand why the foundation wants to neutralise this thing because from their POV it must be pants shittingly scary to have this organisation out there, but ultimately there's no actual way to do it unless you can somehow kill every single one of them instantly, and even then that assumes that the anomalous force behind it will cease once all infected individuals are dead, which is a pretty foolhardy assumption to make. The only reason it hasn't just infected the entire foundation is because they've agreed not to (at least, as far as the foundation knows; it could be possible that the SCP simply lacks the ability to do that I guess).

I know that the article basically says "okay guys, try to think up of a way to end this thing" which implies they're aware of these issues and are trying to work around them, and the tone of the article implies that this is going to take a long time to plan, but something about how it's written puts me off. The entire article is basically detailing how widespread, dangerous and downright insidious this thing is, then the proposal for neutralisation adds to that by saying "seriously guys, this thing is, like, whack", and then it ends with "Yup, let's kill it. Anyway, moving on…"

Just from my point of view there needs to be at least a little bit of discussion (or something), from a pragmatic view, about just how hard this thing would be to kill, and how dangerous it would be to even try. Theoretically this thing could end the foundation the instant it even suspected that the foundation was moving against it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. Keter bureaucracy that seems to have harnessed chaos and the butterfly effect to alter world events to an unknown purpose. I will say that the final decision to neutralize comes as a bit of a surprise given how it's laid out in the request form. I mean, does the Ethics Committee being 2 to 1 against neutralization even mean anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree, but I believe it also needs more gore. Gore makes everything better (specially with som SPC on it)

E: "the adequate gore council squad"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's right, you know. They sit in median strips and tally the number of yellow cars that drive past.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Setting aside issues of whether that's a good idea or not (or if indeed it's playing right into the collective's hands — if all their actions further their goals, surely there's a reason they chose such alarming names), the collapsible in the containment procedures reads to me as though they have such a plan that they are about to imminently put into action, one that you just elide over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ಠ_ಠ

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It occurs to me that it would make complete sense for SCP-1659 to have a Shark Punching Center.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We don't have many thaumiels. It would be refreshing to see one that's not and 001 proposal or SCP-2000.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like an anomalous version of Quantum from the James Bond films.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was dead certain that no one was going to catch that, and lo and behold it's the first comment. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The complete dissolution of the SCP Foundation into a silly parody of itself VS. The neutralization of one of almost 200 keter class objects, and a rather innocuous one at that.

This entire leading paragraph is a fair criticism. As I said upthread, I wrote this with a sense that the Foundation felt the Sword of Damocles over their heads with this anomaly. And, in their view, they are picking between two bad options (I guess I'm stuck on that theme lately). I did try to acknowledge in the article that there are arguments against that, but this skip isn't really "innocuous" as such. It has the potential to do really, really harmful things if it wants to, and we have no understanding of whether it ultimately wants to. But I get where you're coming from.

like, perhaps, making it a thaumiel or something. That would be cool. You'd make a great SCP, get my upvote, and, my nearly eternal appreciation.

This, though. I don't understand this at all. How would making this thaumiel add anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) How does the Foundation have any idea of what its goal are, given how random the actions of its members are?

2) What sort of "broader contextual analysis" could take a huge collection of weird, random behavior and say "yep, that weird stuff is having an effect on this thing and that other thing"?

The entry was written post-Protocol. As part of the Protocol, SCP-1659 must send subjects for research periodically to allow the Foundation to understand it better. They can't cut them up or experiment on them or whatever, but they can interview subjects, gain a wider understanding of what exactly is going on, and research the larger implications. At the beginning, of course, they didn't have the level of understanding they do now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what, I think you're right. I might add something in to cover that. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Er, what? If we really accepted that, we lose. We don't all live in fear that SCP-131 will spontaneously start killing people. If past behavior holds no predictive value, our research is necessarily pointless. Moreover, it's equivalent to admitting that our containment is the equivalent of praying to the gods for rain. The Foundation cannot hold this view, not without relinquishing the notion that it can be a bastion against the irrational.

Hmmm. Now that I think of it, I did put it rather inelegantly. I think I'll tinker with that part. Thanks for calling my attention to it.

Also, I really don't see why the Foundation decided to terminate the members of 1659. It's honestly hard to imagine how that can go well. Short of a simultaneous strike on hundreds of thousands people world-wide (obviously impossible), most will survive. This stands a huge risk to exposing the Foundation, will certainly make it look bad, and probably wouldn't even neutralize the anomaly! After all, if it could generate itself once, it could probably do it again. I mean, I know you have a dark view of the Foundation-verse, but this is defying reason in the name of darkness.

Well, it is hard to imagine it going well, which is why they haven't done it yet. They're still working on the plan, which will be incredibly complex, very difficult, and probably go wrong in unforeseen ways. But they don't have very good options here; they can't really let it run loose (though I think there is actually a case to be made the other way, which I made through the ambassador), and they can't ever achieve a true lockdown. So this is, in their view, the way to go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved that you used diplomacy in order to contain this thing, really cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps it should be: "Widely accepted organizational doctrine holds that past behavior holds relatively little predictive power for anomalies;"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By a vote of 8-5, Overwatch Command authorizes the request. Staff are instructed to begin preparations for neutralization. Object is henceforth classed as a Type K5 Neutralization Hazard; resources and contingency plans as such have been implemented.

See? This is why there should be a halfway point between euclid and keter. Or something past keter. Also, this portrays the foundation as a bunch of idiots, because they're taking on an organization with many times their own influence and expecting to win. They also seem to think it's a secret, when the containment procedures make it obvious that Directorate K has access to information about nearly everything on earth, including, one should assume, their own documentation. What's more, SCP-1659 seems to be able to infect any individual at any time, this implies that SCP-1659 could completely dissolve the foundation by converting every single one of it's members into an instance of SCP-1659γ. All in all, an attempt at neutralization is obviously a bad idea in terms of risk vs. reward, that relationship being: The complete dissolution of the SCP Foundation into a silly parody of itself VS. The neutralization of one of almost 200 keter class objects, and a rather innocuous one at that.

In short, it's a stupid thing for the foundation to try to neutralize this SCP. I don't wan't to downvote, because despite that, it's a good SCP, but I don't want to upvote, because I don't want to encourage the production of SCPs involving the Foundation doing something reckless. So this is a No-vote, unless you do something about those collapsibles, like, perhaps, making it a thaumiel or something. That would be cool. You'd make a great SCP, get my upvote, and, my nearly eternal appreciation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While observed activity of SCP-1659 subjects at the individual level appears to have no logical purpose, broader contextual analysis has revealed that SCP-1659 exhibits a profound ability to affect world commodities market fluctuations, cultural trends (particularly those related to food), real estate development, movement of refugee populations, and, to a limited extent, deployment of military assets.

1) How does the Foundation have any idea of what its goal are, given how random the actions of its members are?

2) What sort of "broader contextual analysis" could take a huge collection of weird, random behavior and say "yep, that weird stuff is having an effect on this thing and that other thing"?

Foundation researchers discovered SCP-1659 in 1983 after an investigation into reports of seventeen unaffiliated individuals chasing stray dogs through the streets of Budapest, Hungary with brooms, mops, and various cleaning implements.

It stretches my suspension of disbelief that the Foundation will investigate anytime people do weird stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why didn't the 9 Mile Station Protocol include a clause stating that SCP-1659 cannot infiltrate the Foundation? Because that seems like a huuuuuuge oversight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly felt the ending came a bit out of nowhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Widely accepted organizational doctrine holds that past behavior holds no predictive power whatsoever for anomalies;

Er, what? If we really accepted that, we lose. We don't all live in fear that SCP-131 will spontaneously start killing people. If past behavior holds no predictive value, our research is necessarily pointless. Moreover, it's equivalent to admitting that our containment is no better than praying to the gods for rain. The Foundation cannot hold this view, not without relinquishing the notion that it can be a bastion against the irrational.

Also, I really don't see why the Foundation decided to terminate the members of 1659. It's honestly hard to imagine how that can go well. Short of a simultaneous strike on hundreds of thousands people world-wide (obviously impossible), most will survive. This stands a huge risk to exposing the Foundation, will certainly make it look bad, and probably wouldn't even neutralize the anomaly! After all, if it could generate itself once, it could probably do it again. I mean, I know you have a dark view of the Foundation-verse, but this is defying reason in the name of darkness.

I do very much like the premise, and the execution is solid (though I really don't buy the subtle reverberations trope, even if I use it too), but the development just sours the piece for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn cliffhangers, why must you be in such a great article. To address those below me, I was assuming they were preparing to resort to something big, something unmentioned as a solution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, great job Kalinin. Well written indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… ElBulli?

ElBulli, Catalonia, Spain?

I knew it. O-o
